name: Aten
description: Aten, also spelled Aton, was the disk of the sun in ancient Egyptian religion, and originally an aspect of Ra. During the Amarna Period (c. 1353–1336 BC), it became the focus of the monolatristic, or arguably monotheistic, religion of Atenism established by Akhenaten. In Akhenaten's religion, Aten was not merely the sun disc but the divine source of all life and energy in the universe. Akhenaten closed the traditional temples and promoted Aten as the supreme and only god to be worshipped. Aten was depicted as the sun disc, with rays emanating from it, often ending in hands offering the ankh (the symbol of life) to Akhenaten and his family. The worship of Aten involved hymns and prayers praising its life-giving properties. After Akhenaten's death, the traditional polytheistic religion was restored, and the worship of Aten was suppressed, deemed heretical, and associated with the chaos of the Amarna period. The concept of Aten represents a unique and controversial chapter in Egyptian religious history, marking a shift towards a more abstract and potentially monotheistic form of worship before the return to traditional beliefs.
subtitle: The Solar Disk, Source of Life

keyMyths.title: Akhenaten's Revelation
keyMyths.description: Akhenaten claimed a divine revelation from Aten, leading to the establishment of Atenism and the construction of Akhetaten as the new capital. This marked a significant departure from traditional Egyptian polytheism.

keyMyths.title: Hymn to the Aten
keyMyths.description: The 'Great Hymn to the Aten' praises Aten as the creator and sustainer of all life, emphasizing its universal reach and benevolence. It provides insight into the theological beliefs of Atenism.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Atenism
keyMyths.description: After Akhenaten's death, the worship of Aten was suppressed, temples were dismantled, and the traditional pantheon was restored under Tutankhamun. Aten was branded as a heretical deity.

sources.author: Donald B. Redford
sources.description: A detailed biography of Akhenaten and an analysis of the Amarna period.
sources.text: A detailed biography of Akhenaten and an analysis of the Amarna period.

sources.author: Jan Assmann
sources.work: The Search for God in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Explores the concept of monotheism and its potential manifestation in Atenism.
sources.text: Explores the concept of monotheism and its potential manifestation in Atenism.
